comments.title: A Wiccan
comments.content:
Re: your LBRP for polytheists (https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/9828.html)

I've had some thoughts I wanted to run by you on adapting this to a Wiccan(-ish) context. I realize that Wiccans have so far gotten by using the classical version of the LBRP, but I'd like one more theologically-appropriate.

My ideas:

1. For PATRON, "Aradia" or "Ceridwen" etc. (depending on tradition) would obviously work, but maybe just "the (TRIPLE) GODDESS" followed by "my Lady" instead of "my patron goddess" to avoid the repetition. (Alternatively, "Cernnunos" or "the (HORNED) GOD, my Lord.")

2. Using the Four Powers of the Magus as the four-letter divine names, especially for those who haven't chosen a traditional pantheon. Calling on the Four Powers seems like it would be thematically-appropriate for Wicca, and preferable to a mix-and-match approach.

"KNOW," "WILL," and "DARE" -- the bare infinitive forms of the verbs -- all helpfully have four letters, and "keep silent" can maybe be replaced with "MUTE" or even "HUSH," which can mean the same thing. (There's also "STFU," of course, but, uh...)

Alternatively, keep the "to" and "silent" but only vibrate the four letter words: "to KNOW, to WILL, to DARE, to KEEP silent"?

Do these ideas seem reasonable, or would they mess with the ritual too much and blow up in my face?

replies.title: Re: A Wiccan
replies.content: My guess is that it would be underpowered because you're not calling on deities in the four quarters, but you could certainly give it a try and see how well it works.

comments.content:
Hello JMG,

1. How do I know when a repeated invocation has actually attracted something and “inhabited” a visualization or telesmatic image, as opposed to the image remaining an empty projection of the imagination? How dramatic can one expect the visualizations to become once they actually attract the attention of the right entities?

2. How much of a role does raw intelligence and innate talent play in magic? What differentiates someone like John Dee or Michael Scot from the average person?

3. Would it be possible for an aspirant to self-initiate himself all the way to adepthood by performing only the LBRP, MP, discursive meditation and divination, skipping all the other GD grade work and initiation ceremonies?

4. From an occult perspective, why do certain people gain weight easily and others not, while eating practically the same diet? Is there any way to influence this process through ritual or other means?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) That depends on fine details of the development of your inner senses, which have at least as much to do with what you perceive as does the degree of presence of inner entities.

2) A willingness to do the work is more important than any other factor. I don't know Scot's biography well, but Dee achieved what he did by devoting extravagant amounts of time and energy to magical study and practice.

3) No. That will take you a certain distance, but not to adepthood.

4) A galaxy of different factors influence body weight, and how much food you eat has very little to do with it. I know of no occult way to control body weight -- if there was one, given the number of people who are frankly not sane about their weight, so many people would have taken up magic that the skeptics would have been brushed aside years ago.

replies.title: (no subject)

2) When the entities show up, of course.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
JMG, I must differ. I think how much you eat has a LOT to do with it, not only because no fat person was ever liberated from a concentration camp, but I have lost a lot of weight from no other factor than the normal waning of appetite that comes with age. I think the difficulty with weight loss is because most of us don’t realize how little food it takes to keep you going—I certainly didn’t—and because most of us don’t realize how much we actually eat—I certainly didn’t. The Hershey’s kisses on the secretary ‘s desk, the wad of ham you ate while leaning into the fridge to see what there was to eat, the spoonful of leftovers you ate after dinner, the grapes beginning to look tired so you finished them, etc etc etc—who, even dieters writing down everything they eat, is going to remember those little things at the end of the day?

From my experience, I suggest that people having difficulty losing weight cut their food intake by 25% for a week, and see what happens. Good luck! (As best as I can figure, I eat about 60% less than I did before I lost the 70 pounds, but I think cutting your food intake by that much might be quite difficult if you still have a young or middle-aged person’s appetite. So start with 25% and get used to that before moving onward.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: A minority of people can decrease their weight by restricting intake. The great majority cannot. People have been starving themselves for decades with no noticeable result -- other than misery and a higher rate of certain cardiac problems -- and I'm not going to encourage people to keep doing that.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Well, that was what I think is going wrong. We think we’re starving ourselves, but we are eating more than we think.

Natural loss of appetite isn’t miserable, I noticed. I was and am perfectly happy as far as food goes. I wonder if the misery of dieting isn’t so stressful it makes your body hang on to the fat. Having what little to eat that I wanted wasn’t stressful at all. Even if all I wanted all day was a glass of milk, I was perfectly happy. YOUR ATTENTION FOLKS: I do NOT recommend trying to live on milk if you are older than 6 months. But if you can figure out what you really want to eat and confine yourself to that, losing weight may be less stressful. You may notice some changes in your tastes. I usually want butter now; before, I didn’t care either way. Good luck!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If your appetite decreases naturally, that's one thing. If you try to force yourself to eat less than you want, that's something else again. I know rather too many people whose appetite did not decrease with age -- and some who had that happen and who still gained weight. Thus my point -- your individual experience is not a rule for everyone, and in a society that is deeply neurotic about body weight, the kind of talk you're engaging in here could encourage some poor soul on the edge of anorexia to tip over into that condition.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:

I suspect industrialized food turns to fat more easily than does real food. The bigger Big Ag got, the bigger its captives got as well.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Don't forget level of physical of activity as well as changes in metabolism as we age. Modern society bombards us with 'entertainment' inducing us to stay put on our couches and provides us with sugary snacks while we watch the gyrating colorful figures on the screen. There's a significant source of pudge right there.

For myself I was always around one hundred pounds soaking wet for most of my early years irregardless of what I ate. Once I went through menopause that all changed. I slowly but inexorably went up to 120 lbs which I know isn't really a whole lot but it seemed that way to me. I have stayed that way ever since. I walk almost daily, cut back on the snacking and do some weight bearing exercises to combat osteopenia but the scales stubbornly refuse to budge from 120. I guess this is the new normal for me (sigh).

replies.title: (no subject)

As a single data point, I've found that paying attention to the etheric qualities of my food has led to a small but consistent decrease in the amount of fat I'm carrying. (I'm not orthorexic about it — that's also miserable — but I consider it as a factor.) Ymmv, I have a lot of skinny genetic relatives, etc; not everyone is likely to have the same experience. Fat is on the rise among mammals generally, even lab marmosets with highly controlled diets, and nobody knows why. On the other hand, good fresh food is its own reward, especially during raspberry season.

My best guess is that food has been for me a major source of close daily encounters with nonhuman energies, and I seem to need a lot of that. Industrialized, engineered, packaged food has been miserable in that regard. It feels like I was chasing some sort of faint remnant vitality in those Big Ag foods, the way someone who's slightly deficient in a vitamin might intuitively eat a great deal of whatever food is available.

comments.title: Aspects of Gods in SOP, Ogham Check, Gender in Sun Symbolism, Occult Study, Stanislav Grof
comments.content:
Good Evening!

As always, thanks much for sharing your expertise and wisdom. I've asked five questions below, roughly in order of how much of a priority they are for me, but I'm working on not being quite so presumptuous here, so please feel free to answer as few or as many as it moves you to do so.

Also as always, I welcome thoughts from the commentariat as well.


  1. When invoking the elements for the SOP, is it appropriate to use different aspects/manifestations of the same God or Goddess for different elements? My specific case: for the SOP with Norse Deities, you suggest Freyja for Fire/South and Frigga for Earth/North. Working with a more Anglo-Saxon pantheon, and based on some of my own prayer, I have been worshiping a single Goddess that has aspects that cover both what Freyja and Frigga rule in the Norse pantheon. I'm guessing this is an "experiment and find out"/"pray for some guidance" situation, but I was curious if there were other factors to consider.

  2. I'm working on learning Ogham divination as part of working through /The Druid Magic Handbook/. As a check on learning, I thought I might ask if I'm way off base on my take on today's draw (note that I drew in the order Knowledge, Power, Peace, but I've presented them in left-to-right order below):
    1. Knowledge: Onn (Reversed)
    2. Peace: Coll (Reversed)
    3. Power: Huath

    My take: Over-confidence in how I approach/think about things has led me to an intellectual/creative block. If I use this as an opportunity to reassess, think, and try new approaches, some good may come of it.

    Obviously I know there's a lot that's context-dependent, so I'm asking less for "please tell me what this means!" and more "are there any concepts or principles I should be applying to my interpretation that I'm missing".

  3. The Cosmic Doctrine, Eliphas Levi, GD sources, and about every other Indo-European mythology tend to describe the sun with masculine symbolism, but the Germanic myths describe the sun as feminine (the Goddess Sunna). Is this likely to be a problem in magical work? Practical example: I'm currently doing the Celtic Cross opening to the SOP and invoking Odin and Baldur for the head and solar plexus centers while visualizing solar energy, but when I do morning, noon, and evening Sun Salutations, I address them to Sunna.

  4. Besides daily discursive meditation, do you have any study tips for learning things occult? Any details you're willing to share on whether/how you take notes, use flashcards, mnemonic techniques, and so on would be much appreciated.

  5. I've already been plenty greedy tonight, so I'm happy to push this one off to another day: any thoughts on Stanislav Grof and/or his "holotropic breathing" technique?



Many thanks,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Aspects of Gods in SOP, Ogham Check, Gender in Sun Symbolism, Occult Study, Stanislav Grof
replies.content:
Thanks very much!

Should you (or others) be interested, Stanislav Grof is a psychological researcher who was one of the earliest to use LSD in a research/clinical setting. He became convinced that psychedelics offered a way to explore and map human consciousness, but later came to believe that other methods could be used to access the "non-ordinary states of consciousness" that helped find some of those nooks and crannies.

"Holotropic Breathing" is a method where you breathe in and out "as deeply as you can as fast as you can" while laying down with your eyes closed, ideally with a trained sitter next to you talking you through it and making sure you don't hurt yourself. In sessions around an hour long, it's supposed to have similar effects to psychedelics, and with a trained sitter/guide, some folks have had profound therapeutic responses to it.

It's a method/area of research that was more interesting to me when I had a primarily materialist point of view, but strikes me as potentially risky and/or unnecessary from an occult point of view.

replies.title: Re: Aspects of Gods in SOP, Ogham Check, Gender in Sun Symbolism, Occult Study, Stanislav Grof
replies.content:
As part of my ongoing psychospiritual journey, I participated in a Holotropic Breathwork workshop led by Dr. Grof about 20 years ago. It was interesting, but personally I didn't experience significant insights or transformation of consciousness.

Another participant went into what appeared to be a full-blown spiritual emergency (to use a Grofian term) and the trained sitters present at the workshop had their hands full trying to navigate her out of it. I remember hearing her anguished screams. She was escorted to a private room and they spent the better part of the afternoon working with her. So I can validate your concern about the potential risks.

replies.title: Re: Aspects of Gods in SOP, Ogham Check, Gender in Sun Symbolism, Occult Study, Stanislav Grof
replies.content:
JMG gives a recommendation for the elements
[Error: Irreparable invalid markup ('<a [...] https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/57125.html?thread>') in entry. Owner must fix manually. Raw contents below.]

JMG gives a recommendation for the elements <a href="https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/59968.html?thread=4929344#cmt4929344>here</a> and for the non-Celtic Cross opening <a href="https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/57125.html?thread=5030181#cmt5030181">here</a>.

Reprinted here for convenience:

For the opener: "I'd invoke Odin, Frigga, and Baldr in the opening."

For the elements:
"I would use the following Norse deities in the SoP:

Air/East: Thor the thunder and storm god
Water/West: Njord the god of fertility and the sea
Fire/South: Freyja the queen of the Valkyries and warrior goddess
Earth/North: Frigga the queen of the Aesir and spinner of fates
Spirit Above: Odin, seated on Hlidskjalf at the summit of the worlds
Spirit Below: Fjorgyn the earth goddess, wife of Odin and mother of Thor

You could swap out Heimdall for Njord if you prefer, or have Tyr in place of Thor, but these are the ones I would use -- mind you, I'd pour each of them a horn of ale first, and ask their permission to invoke them in the ritual!

The thing to keep in mind is that the elemental symbolism doesn't have to be exact. The seven stations of the SoP -- the Seven Cantrefs, as they're called in a certain tradition of Druid lore -- are more than just the elements, and there's some room for flexibility when assigning them to deities."

comments.title: Prayer thanks
comments.content:
Two weeks ago, I announced a prayer request on behalf of my 17 yo daughter Jenny who dislocated her shoulder in summer camp. This has been a recurring problem and seems to be getting worse.

This comment is 1): to thank everyone who did pray for her, and 2): to inform you of the outcome to date.

Surprisingly, she had the best outcome one could hope for, short of a miraculous cure. The shoulder MRI revealed no major changes or abnormalities. Even her old surgical shoulder repair was intact; a surprise finding, as we had been told the repair was destroyed 2 months after her original surgery in 2016. Clinically, her shoulder remains extremely lax and unstable, and her orthopedist re-iterates my fear that she may need a total surgical reconstruction of not only her left, but BOTH shoulders; but this can potentially be averted if Jenny gets serious about her daily shoulder exercises, which she is prone to slack off on, because they are “boring”. She needs muscle tone to substitute for connective tissue tightness. We are getting her set up again with her physical therapist.

Again, my thanks and blessings to you all. And thank you JMG for posting my appeal.

—Lunar Apprentice

replies.title: Re: Prayer thanks

Not so much a magical answer (and certainly no replacement for medical advice, etc, etc) but you may want to look into Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome (also known as EDS) - it's a genetic disorder which can cause frequent dislocation due to weaker connective tissue. It's also remarkably common among young women these days and relatively difficult to be diagnosed with as there's no specific genetic test and a lot of doctors (and physical therapists, for that matter) are unaware of it. However there are a lot of resources online which may be of help.

Hope your daughter is doing well.

comments.title: Tarot Meditation
comments.content:
JMG:

I discovered something interesting when I was doing the meditation on the minor arcana as part of chapter three in 'Learning Ritual Magic." That is, the part where you are supposed to visualize things having to do with the seasons and feel their energy.

Everything that is usually associated with spring, I associate with fall. A chill in the air and changing leaves gives me a feeling of new beginnings, ambition, sexual energy, and possibility. Spring on the other hand feels like a time to wrap things up before the summer. (Summer heat makes me feel lethargic.)

Meditating about winter on the other hand, feels weird because I can visualize winter things like cold, pink skies in a snowstorm, the cold earth, sleeping trees, and so on. But if I add lights and Christmas songs if feel an inner warmth and deep contentment (throw in some homebrew with mulling spices and it's heaven). But, take away the lights and Christmas music and it feels a lot more bleak.

I think this might be related to the way the school year works, starting in fall and ending in summer. I write not to ask a question but to relate my experience.

Hey! Same thing here... Though I love the bleakness... And LOTR starts in the fall, too...
Rhydlyd

replies.content: I drag around in summer too.  I hate summer.  Heat, humidity, harsh glaring sunlight, yard work, arctic air conditioning, bugs, noise—yuck.  The only good thing I can say about summer is I don’t have to worry about slipping on ice.

replies.title: Re: Tarot Meditation
replies.content: The same for me, too!  I really, really hate summers--too much light, too much warmth, too many people out doing too many things too energetically, too much ambient noise, too much activity throughout the entire world of Nature.  Fall and winter are my favorite seasons; spring is tolerable.  (I'm a pretty extreme introvert, too.  This feels like another side of one and the same die.)

replies.title: Re: Tarot Meditation
replies.content: What seems odd to me is that the year always seems to 'start' in mid-to-late August for me as well, although it's been [more than I will admit] years since I've been to school... of course Lammas is around the corner and that's as good of a time to start a year as any... 1 January, OTOH, is just plain weird...

replies.title: Re: Tarot Meditation
replies.content:
You know why we start the year in January right? The Roman's were losing a war in the Iberian peninsula and they needed a new general, but being Roman's they couldn't just break the rule on appointments, so they moved up the start of the new year.

Pretty much every culture everywhere does new year in the spring or the fall. Either planting or harvest. We should ditch the Roman convention.

comments.title: Tiny occult publishing project
comments.content:
I am teaching myself basic bookbinding. For a learning project, I would like to print up a short work on an occult or esoteric subject and bind it as a pamphlet, with possible sale in mind should there prove to be an audience for it. Something no longer than Trithemius’ “On Drawing Spirits Into Crystals,” or a bit shorter, would be about the right length for the scale I have in mind.

Any work I print for possible sale must be in the public domain, and any translation thereof must also be so. I’m also thinking of selected Orphic or Homeric hymns, such as the translations of Thomas Taylor. Or perhaps it would not be too great a mutilation of a longer work to excerpt some passages from it, such as Plato’s description of Atlantis from the Timaeus and the Critias..? replies.title: Re: Tiny occult publishing project

replies.title: Re: Tiny occult publishing project

There is this useful piece of cosmology from Sallustius, which is mentioned now and then on MM, beginning at: https://archive.org/details/in.ernet.dli.2015.211223/page/n241/mode/2up

If you are typing and printing or typesetting the text yourself might I advise caution --to have your work checked if your 18C English is rusty-- in reproducing text exactly as you find it on the web; in Taylor's hymns on Sacred Texts for instance there are errors based on incorrect transliteration from editions in old typeface to contemporary fonts.

comments.title: Some questions on divination as a daily practice
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

some questions

(1) Can astrology be used as a "training" oracle like the Tarot, with its daily 3 card spread, and checking against the days events?

The conceptual difficulty I encounter is that in a span of 12-18 hours, only the Moon moves significantly enough from day to day.

In other words, if you take the span of, say 3 days, most planets don't move all that much, and usually don't transit over natal chart points. Horary doesn't seem to quite fit for *daily* divination, but I suppose one *could* cast a horary chart every day with the “What do I most need to understand about the events of this day?” question. If you do this at roughly the same time everyday, you'd get roughly similar charts, modulo the Moon. Is this a good idea (to develop intuition via daily divination practice, using astrology instead of Tarot ?

2) In the daily 3 card spread, you seem to recommend a "first card is me, second is the situation, third is the resolution" structure. I understand the latter two but have some difficulty wrapping my head around the "myself" card. What would it mean for say the Two Of Cups vs (say) the Heirphant to come up in the "me" position? Does it say something of what I do that day? what I feel? a role I play that day? My confusion is that I think it is the same "me" that goes through successive days. What do different cards indicate then? A concrete example would really help.


3) Due to my Tai Chi practice, which provides me immense benefits, given that I have put a few years into it, I'm presently not taking the Golden Dawn path (as in LRM etc) to avoid energy clashes.

That said, I've been reading a few of your books (RLM, CGD, DH etc) , and a meta pattern I detect is that the challenge for the beginner would be in taking up and sustaining a relentless practice of *some* occult practise over time.

Bardon's IIH shows the same structure, and puts this 'relentless practice' aspect right up front, specifically asking the practitioner to build habits of not acting out of/being driven by subconscious impulses and complexes, and I suspect this is why most beginners get stuck on his first two steps for years! That's a hard "first step" though potentially rewarding if you can manage it.

Consequently, I'm thinking of skipping the ritual/energy work for now, focusing on just the divination and meditation practices and focusing on getting 'relentless practice' of these two nailed before re looking at energetics/ritual. And possibly add some language learning _ Sanskrit or Latin - or daily prayer/devotional practice, as a third practice, instead of ritual, to form a trivium - again with focus on getting the ability to practice relentlessly, no matter whether I feel like doing it on a particular day or not, whether I have results or not .

My question: Divination + meditation should go well with tai chi, and not cause energy clashes. Correct? I'm fairly sure meditation wouldn't (clash), just checking with you about the Tarot/astrology.


Thanks in advance, as always.

replies.title: Re: Some questions on divination as a daily practice
replies.content:
1) Yes. The secret to doing astrology as a daily practice is to track transits across your natal chart. On any average day the Moon will make anything from two to a dozen aspects to different natal planets, and there's a good chance that the Sun, Mercury, and Venus are also making aspects to your natal chart that will change in a day or so; the slower planets also make aspects, which take their time changing, but there again, you can track them. Thus every day you get a changing pattern of forces to interpret.

2) The "me" card can indicate your mood, your state of consciousness, your state of health -- anything that affects you from within.

3) That should be fine. It's the ritual work, and especially the internal energy workings, that cause clashes.

replies.title: Re: Some questions on divination as a daily practice
replies.content: Do you know any good sources on how to read these daily transits? I can find sources on the big picture outer planet transits, but haven't seen much on the slow moving ones.

replies.content: The last book I know of to be published on the subject appeared before the Second World War. I'm planning on writing a new book on that shortly.

replies.content:

It has everything, and Robert Hand is a good an atsrologer as you could want to read.

You can bless yourself with it, but it is put on your body, not drunk. Generally, however, it is sprinkled on what you're trying to bless.

The recipe for holy water that I have used in the past is as follows. I took it from the book "Exorcism: A Manual for Casting Out Evil Spirits" by Frank J. MacHovec.
-----
You will need:
- Salt
- Fresh water

Exorcist [EX]: Our help is in the name of the Lord
ALL: Who made Heaven and Earth
EX [over the salt]: God's creation, I cast out the demon from you by the living God. [make sign of cross over the salt] By the true God. [make sign of the cross]
By God who ordered you to be thrown into the waterspring by Eliseus to heal it of its barrenness. May you be a purified salt, a means of health for those who believe, a medicine for body and soul for all who make use of you. May all evil fancies of the foul fiend, his malice and cunning, be driven afar from the place where you are sprinkled. And let every unclean spirit be repulsed by Him who is coming to judge both the living and the dead and the world by fire.
ALL: Amen.
EX [over the water]: God's creation, water, I cast out the demon from you in the name of God the Father Almighty. [make sign of the cross over it] In the name of Jesus Christ. [make sign of the cross] His Son, our Lord, and in the power of the Holy Spirit. [make sign of the cross]
May you be a purified water, empowered to drive afar all power of the enemy; in fact, to root out and banish the enemy himself, along with his fallen angels. We ask this through the power of our Lord Jesus Christ, who is coming to judge the living and the dead and the world by fire.
ALL: Amen.
EX: Let us pray: O God, who for man's welfare established the most wonderful mysteries in the substance of water, hearken to our prayer and pour forth your blessing. [make sign of the cross] On this element now being prepared with various purifying rites. May this creation of yours, when used with your mysteries and endowed with your grace, serve to cast out demons and to banish disease. May everything that this water sprinkles in the homes and gatherings of the faithful be delivered from all that is unclean and hurtful; let no breath of contagation hover there, no taint of corruption; let all the wiles of the lurking enemy come to nothing. By the sprinkling of this water may everything opposed to safety and peace of the occupants of these homes be banished, so that in calling on your holy name they may know the well-being they desire, and be protected from every peril; through Christ, our Lord.
ALL: Amen.
EX: [pour salt into water in the form of the cross, once only, as you say] May this salt and water be mixed together in the name of the Father and of the Son and the Holy Spirit.
ALL: Amen.
EX: May the Lord be with you.
ALL: May He also be with you.
EX: Let us pray: God, source of irresistible might and king of an invincible realm, the everglorious conqueror; who restrains the force of the adversary, silences the uproar of his rage, and valiantly subdues his wickedness; in awe and humility we beg you, Lord, to regard with favor these creations of salt and water, to let the light of your kindness shine upon it, and to hallow it with the dew of your mercy, so that wherever it is sprinkled and your holy name is invoked, every assault of the unclean spirit may be baffled, and all dread of the serpent's venom be cast out. To us who entreat your mercy grant that the Holy Spirit may be with us wherever we may be, through Christ, our Lord.
ALL: Amen.
[Exorcist sprinkles self, those present, and the place]

If you are talking about Weltz or Croft Orgonite, that was probably me.

"Classic" or "Weltz" orgonite is simply metal wire and filings inside of an organic epoxy like BondoTM[*], and by all accounts behaves the same way that classic orgone shooters do.

Croft orgonite is that with a small amount of quartz crystal in it. It is alleged to do what regular orgonite does, and filter/transform DOR, including DOR transmitted by ambient electromagnetic waves. According to some of the people that make it, its properties can be enhanced by doing various things during its curing time.

Verifying the alleged properties of Croft orgonite would take someone skilled in etheric clairvoyance who hasn't been biased by the marketing materials of the orgonite community.

[*] actual metal and actual epoxy, in roughly a 50/50 mixture for both. Glass, plastic and plaster don't have the right properties.

replies.title: Re: Tattoos and the Occult
replies.content:
My father's step-father left home in Copenhagen for good and went to sea in 1899, when he was 13 years old. He had a plain sailor's tattoo on one forearm; it was just his initials: F E P. Sailor's tattoos of that sort are put there to help identify the sailor's body if he drowns and isn't recovered for a while. (Sea creatures start with the face when they eat a drowned sailor, so the body soon becomes unrecognizable without such a tattoo.)

Back in whaling-ship days, tattoos were mostly worn by the Polynesian seamen. My father's maternal grandfather was a whaling-man until he jumped ship in San Francisco in 1869, when he was 18.

So I never wanted to get any sort of tattoo, landsman that I am.

comments.title: Egregors Again
comments.content:
Good morning!

I've been pondering egregors and their effects on the individuals connected to them. The specific egregor that triggered this line of thought is that of the Boy Scouts of America. I joined Scouting at the age of seven and progressed through to being an Eagle Scout at seventeen. Throughout it was a very positive experience and highly influential on my current values system. I am quite proud of the work I did during that decade of my life.

The revelation of the sexual abuse and the bowing to wokeness has greatly disturbed me. The only abuse I knew of in my admittedly small and rural Council was a father and son and the father committed suicide when it was revealed publicly. Beyond that, every leader I encountered seemed to be genuinely caring and concerned about the welfare of us Scouts.

Knowing that this is part of the Boy Scout's history (and they insist that the vast majority happened in the distant past) has led to me wonder how that has affected me and the millions of other boys who did benefit from the positive aspects of Scouting over the years. Are we still part of the problem? Part of my pondering is that the Boy Scouts now have troops for girls as well and I have considered enrolling my daughter who will be eligible in a couple of years.

Can an organization ever remove a stain as deep as this from its collective karma? Can I, as an Eagle, do something to help repair the damage of and to an organization that gave me so much? Would my daughter be better off somewhere else?

Thank you for all you do for our community in sharing your knowledge and wisdom

-Wounded Eagle

replies.title: Re: Egregors Again
replies.content: Every egregor is a mixed bag, and Scouting's egregor is better than most -- not least because the organization has owned up to the problem and set out to make amends for it. I'd encourage you to affirm the positive aspects of the egregor, and consider getting involved in it as an adult, so that other kids can have the same kind of positive experience you did.

replies.title: Re: Egregors Again
replies.content:
Thank you for bringing this up. I, too, am an Eagle Scout and found scouting to be incredibly beneficial in my life. My father was an assistant scout master (he and my mother had both been den leaders through cub scouts) and it gave him, my brother, and I a lot of really great times together. I never once heard of anything immoral happening in my entire journey through scouting from tiger cubs on up. When I was in the BSA (early to late 90s) we even had to regularly watch cringey films on sexual abuse, how to identify it and how to report it. I was even an order of the arrow member who had to swear all kinds of oaths of silence - but those oaths never concealed any form of degeneracy or abuse. I loved my whole experience in scouting! It taught me so much and instilled in me so much confidence that I did not have before being a part of it. It really is heartbreaking what sorts of atrocities occurred and (perhaps like you) I feel some kind of weird guilt for having been part of something that is now tainted, despite being innocent of any of its wrongdoing. I cherish those memories more and more as I grow older and feel so fortunate to have had the experiences I did. I am so conflicted about how to square that away with these awful revelations about the organization as a whole. It’s a tension that we must not look away from, though, I feel.

Today, I am glad to be a 32nd degree Freemason and to share in that “egregore”, also with my father. Perhaps such institutions are waning, but I am grateful that I got to live at a time when I at least could still know something of their benevolent potentials firsthand.

comments.title: Grounding and Centering
comments.content:
In another MM, you mentioned that grounding and centering were neo-pagan practices. What do magicians do instead. And is grounding and centering some sort of bastardized practice from someplace else like the New Age?

Sleepless Purple Manatee

replies.title: Re: Grounding and Centering
replies.content:
Those terms show up a lot in a neo-pagan context, but they aren't exclusive to that particular stream. Grounding, also called rooting, is an extremely common practice in various martial arts. The basic premise is that in order to fully connect with the earth, everything needs to be released and allowed to align properly with it. Centering exists in a Buddhist context, and I believe it has also been taken up by certain Roman Catholics as well. In the former tradition, the idea is that if you catch yourself overly wrapped up in some phenomena or train of thought, and are identifying to strongly with it, you put your attention on the energy center in the middle of the abdomen to calm down, then allow it to expand outwards to be all encompassing. This may be supplemented by breath techniques.

Most systems of magic use some kind of cross ritual; for example: cabalistic cross in GD, elemental cross in DMH. Crossing oneself in the Christian fashion can also be used. After a period of practice, it is possible to induce a similar return to equilibrium by simply repeating the verbal formula used with the ritual, possibly accompanied by visualization. Not good for formal ritual, but very useful when subtlety is desired. In cabalistic terms, grounding is about making sure that Malkuth is energized by the divine light, centering is about returning the consciousness to Tiphareth.

Personally, I regularly lose my temper with both new-agers and neo-pagans, so this should be taken with a grain of salt; but I take it as a given that they are bastardized borrowings from a better organized tradition. The neo-pagan instructions I've seen on the subject were shockingly bad.

replies.title: Re: Grounding and Centering
replies.content: Centering was originally a New Thought practice. Lida A. Churchill's The Magic Seven (1901) devotes its entire first chapter to centering. Grounding seems to come from theater and dance techniques. I haven't found time to document it solidly yet, but I expect it may go back as far as Genevieve Stebbins and her training courses. In Wicca and Witchcraft they seem first to have been interoduced by Starhawk out in California, and have no place in any earlier witchy traditions. From Starhawk they spread thouughout the New Age scene.

replies.title: Re: Grounding and Centering
replies.content: Since I've never done the Neopagan trip, I don't know what Neopagans use grounding and centering for, so I'm not at all sure which magical practices, if any, cover the same ground.

comments.title: Orgone energy
comments.content: I've seen references to orgone energy and how it's similar to terms like prana and chi...if that's the case, what is the nature of "deadly orgone energy"?

replies.title: Re: Orgone energy
replies.content:
The life force can become contaminated with harmful influences. In feng-shui you'll hear talk about sha, which is chi that has taken on a harmful form and needs to be deflected away from your home. In some of the subtler internal martial arts, doing harmful things to the other guy's chi is a basic element of the fighting method.

DOR ("deadly orgone radiation") is Wilhelm Reich's term for a particularly nasty form of this, which was created by putting radioactive material in an orgone accumulator. (He was trying to make the material less dangerous, and the experiment blew up in his face.) Its exact characteristics are not well understood, as it's very dangerous to work with.

comments.title: Promissory Spirituality/Occultism
comments.content:
Dear JMG & Co.,

Among PSI researchers and proponents there is often a snide remark made about the aspects of existence that materialism fails to explain: that materialists ask us to bear with them while they figure out how to explain those parts of reality within the materialist paradigm - that they expect us to accept “promissory materialism.” We are asked, in other words, to assume materialism will prove itself sufficient to explain certain phenomenon in due time - please don’t go looking for explanations elsewhere. Stay tuned!

One of the things I appreciate about the scientific method is that it enables people to “see for yourself.” We do not have to rely on the teachings of an authority to learn about reality as defined by said authority.

As I consider my many years now of (mis-)adventures(?) in the spiritual marketplace, reading, studying, considering various world views and ideas, and midlife takes hold of me, I am faced with the question of what I actually know and what I have actually learned from all this reading, studying, contemplating, etc. The sad answer is, “Not much.” For many years the “thrill of the chase” was enough of an excuse to keep at it. The carrot of spiritual achievement and experience was just a few inches out ahead of nose. I understand that reading, studying, contemplating is often said not to be enough. That one must take up various practices to go that one essential step further and really “know” for oneself. Perhaps within the scientific metaphor this would be like reading about science experiments without doing any. Magic is not the only offering in the spiritual marketplace, however, and having listened to the experiences and examined the quality of life demonstrated by adherents and believers in many different streams, what I see from a short distance is… not a lot different than the life I experience everyday, or perhaps a lot worse! Some of these “experiments” take quite a long time. Come at considerable material and monetary costs. Some are downright creepy! To me, it seems a lot to ask of a regular Joe to “Stay tuned!” while one takes the many months or years to undertake one of these long-term sets of exercises when one is doing the tasks as much or more for the sake of seeing if the whole thing is based in any truth as much as for the purported “results.” It strikes me as a form of promissory spirituality or occultism, in this case.

I’m on board with the “proof is in the pudding” kind of mindset. However, the pudding that I feast on during my day to day experience after these years of reading, studying, contemplating and (occasionally) practicing is a thin gruel. What experiences I’ve had have come unbidden and before my plunge into these waters. Nothing seems to explain them: materialism, magic, new age spirituality, traditional religions. When I was younger and pursuing things within a Christian framework, I had many older adults tell me, “Don’t throw the baby out with the bath water!” when I would point out issues with the theology or regarding matters of historicity. However, in those dark moments in the course of my searching, when I am most honest with myself, I see no baby anywhere. It is all bath water - and very murky and overly re-used bath water, at that!

To be fully honest, I have long listened to what you (JMG) have to say because you strike me as having integrity and as being very sincere, and I entertain the idea that you have a capacity to see into certain facets of reality that I do not have the capability of seeing into: The same as I trust my eye doctor to see things in my eyes that I cannot see. I have extended this same willingness to suspend disbelief to other folks as well. However, there is no congruent overlap amongst all the “teachers” I listen to. I am left having to acknowledge that someone, somewhere, is mistaken - and I often have the hunch that that person is me!

As I enter the waters of midlife I hunger for my own experience. My own relationship with the “Unseen.” My days frequenting the bookstores, curiosity shops, and blogs have begun to wane. I wish to become a “spiritual adult” now and abide in my own knowledge of things.

If you had to give people like me a single bit of advice or a direction to go, which would put to rest this whole conundrum, what would you suggest? How can we “grow up” spiritually in a way that does not kick the can down the road endlessly?

Sincere thanks.

replies.title: Re: Promissory Spirituality/Occultism
replies.content: Reading, studying, and the rest of it is like reading the menu at a restaurant. Until you sit down at a table, order a meal, eat it all, and digest it, yes, you'll be standing outside looking at the menu and wondering why reading it doesn't satisfy your hunger. That said, you, and only you, can decide to go into that restaurant, or another, and actually have a meal. Nobody else can make that decision for you.

replies.title: Re: Promissory Spirituality/Occultism
replies.content:
"As I enter the waters of midlife I hunger for my own experience. My own relationship with the “Unseen.” My days frequenting the bookstores, curiosity shops, and blogs have begun to wane. I wish to become a “spiritual adult” now and abide in my own knowledge of things."

I was moved by your question. It brought to mind Dante's Divine Comedy, where he finds himself in midlife in a dark forest, having lost his way.

It seems to me that your question contains within itself the seed of its own answer.

You alone can nurture that seed. The process of becoming a spiritual adult takes time and patience, solitude and turning inward. The conundrum will not disappear, but you may face it and follow where it leads.

Do you keep a journal? Do you remember your dreams? Those could be a place to start, a direction to turn, a practice to develop.

I wish you well.

replies.title: Re: Promissory Spirituality/Occultism
replies.content: Thank you both.

comments.title: safe buried spell removal
comments.content: I belong to a small spiritual community in one of the wings of the fading neopagan umbrella that has owned property for around 30 years. A little under a decade ago, a group of people in our community who had made several contributions to our property broke away, in what was a not exactly amicable divorce. We rebuilt and moved on since then, but one of their contributions was a small outdoor shrine that usually gets used as an individual meditation area, or for rituals for which the main circle would be too big. We had been discussing replacing the central altar of that shrine area for quite some time, and finally did the appropriate rituals and moved the old altar. However, we found several magical artifacts buried under the altar including what appeared to be a witch bottle left by the group who built it. While the area had been ostensible for the use of the whole community which is open to the public, the group that built it (and later left as a unit), had formed their own self-made “initiatory order” and were using that space for their own private rituals. Since they were A: a self contained oathbound group, and B: all left in the bad divorce years ago, there’s no way of knowing the details of their intent. That particular area is also where I do the vast majority of my own daily personal practice since I live nearby (which for me is centered around the LBRP). What would be the appropriate cleanup operation for buried spells left over a decade ago by people you’re not on good terms with in order to render that space safe for use again?

replies.title: Re: safe buried spell removal
replies.content: Take the items, choose another space on the property that isn't used for ritual work -- preferably an isolated corner near running water or a road that leads off the property -- and bury them respectfully there. Then plant that area with herbs that banish negative energy, such as angelica. Then you can go ahead and reconsecrate the shrine area as you see fit, since the old energies have been given a place of their own.

comments.title: Possibilities
comments.content:
If you try to measure a quantum object, say a photon, you always get a "point" as a result, suggesting something like a particle. You can't predict at what position the next hit will be measured but you can give predict areas that are likely to score a hit and areas that never will. The famous Taylor-experiment (double slit with feeble light) and the Jönsson-experiment (double slit with electrons) are prominent examples for this. Collecting all the tiny dots will produce the classical interference pattern which one could derive using a wave-formalism. Both combined indicate that an unobserved quantum object can (in way) be very "large" - in the Wheeler's delayed choice gedanken experiment up to 50000 light years and more, yet it will immediately (!) collapse to a tiny speck at a position corresponding to some probability distribution - once it is measured. And the very measurement process by itself has an enormous influence on the probability distribution (no, that's not the uncertainty principle...).

So with that lengthy background - would it make sense to compare acts of consciousness that go beyond the material plane for example divination or ritual magic but also apparitions and visions and many other things to a kind of measurement process that fixes "something" once I observe it with my senses according to a probability distribution that can be shaped by the state of (collective) consciousness? If I am able to invest a lot of energy (deliberately by training of involuntarily by a very positive or very distressing event) I can cause large shifts in the probability distribution that make "unusual" outcomes more likely. Likewise, if a large group of people invests energy into a certain vision, they do the same which might for example result (as some currently seem to experience for certain questions) in garbled divination results. Or result in no effect at all, which is why it is important to shut the f*!& up from time to time.

I hope I am making sense here. All in all I am not trying to explain magic with quantum physics. I doubt you can. But from what I've read here, what I do know about quantum mechanics and from my limited experiences I see some striking analogy and it could be interesting to see how far it goes.

Cheers,
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: Possibilities
replies.content: Yes, you could think of things that way. It's not a new idea -- I think the first time I heard someone suggest a quantum interpretation of magic was in 1979, and Jack Williamson's novel Darker Than You Think, which was published in 1948, used quantum magic as a plot device.

replies.content:

Stapp, physicist, has written on that matter in "Mind, Matter and Quantum Physics". It doesn't stray as far away from the mainstream paradigm as to cover magic but focuses on the mind-brain-problem.

Cheers,
Nachtgurke

comments.title: Holy Housekeeping!
comments.content:
A funny experience this past weekend. My wife asked me to use my powers of manly brawniness to open up a jar of vinegared peppers which had stayed shut for several years— the lid was rusted over. As my brawniness usually leaves a little bit to be desired, I grunted and pulled and contorted myself in various ways trying to open it to no avail. Then a thought came to me— what if I tried vibrating a deity name while doing it, masking it as a grunt? I call on the gods of the seven cantrevs for my daily SOP, so as this was a matter of strength, I decided to call on Sul. It seems silly writing it up now— such a small, plebeian thing to call on a deity for help with! Except… the instant that I started in with “SU…”, the lid came right off!

So, my question is, what would you classify this kind of small moment of aid from the gods? Practical magic? Domestic invocation?

Also, in your opinion is it still being respectful of the gods to call on them even for very minor things like this? (The result was so instantaneous I am tempted to try this more…)

replies.title: Re: Holy Housekeeping!
replies.content: Be grateful and be sure to thank the deity, and it's fine. A lot of practical magic uses divine names for very simple things.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: I keep trying to reconcile the idea of reincarnation with ancestor worship.  If people are just reborn as someone new, why bother engaging with them as who they used to be? I’m assuming someone reborn would also not be able to bestow blessings on their defendants. But I may have this all wrong!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I get this question about once a month. In most cultures that practice ancestor worship, individual ancestors only get prayers and offerings for a certain period -- in Japan it's 40 years, as I recall -- unless there's reason to think that they're out of incarnation for the long term. Ancestors who have been dead longer than that just receive collective prayers and offerings. Do you see how that makes sense? When they're between lives they get individual prayers, while once they're back in incarnation they don't.
